content.text: What Is a Paradigm Shift?
The philosopher Thomas Kuhn described a
paradigm shift

as a fundamental change in the underlying assumptions of a field — not just incremental improvement, but a new way of framing the problem that makes old solutions obsolete and opens up entirely new possibilities.

The move from AI-as-a-tool to AI-as-an-agent is exactly such a shift. The question changes from "What answer can the model give me?" to "What goal can the model achieve for me?" The human's role changes from driver to delegator. The model's role changes from oracle to actor.
The Shift: From Query to Goal

attrs.alt: Side-by-side comparison: Old paradigm (AI as a tool — human queries, gets answer, human acts) vs New paradigm (AI as an agent — human specifies goal, agent loops autonomously, human reviews result)
attrs.title: The Agentic Paradigm Shift

content.text: In the old paradigm, every action requires a human to ask a question, read the answer, decide what to do, and then do it. The AI is a sophisticated lookup service. In the agentic paradigm, the human specifies a goal — often in a single sentence — and the agent decides how to pursue it: what tools to use, in what order, how to handle errors, and when it is done.
Four Concrete Changes
1. From answers to outcomes.

A tool produces an answer: "Here is a Python function that sorts a list." An agent produces an outcome: the function is written, the test file is created, both are committed to the repository, and the CI pipeline turns green. The deliverable is not text — it is a changed state of the world.
2. From single-turn to multi-step.

A query-response cycle is one turn. An agentic task may take 5 turns or 500, each turn building on the observation from the last. The agent maintains state across steps — what has been tried, what worked, what is left to do — rather than starting fresh from a blank context.
3. From passive to active.
A queried model waits to be asked. An agent can schedule itself, monitor external events, send notifications, call APIs, and initiate actions without human prompting for each step. It has
initiative
— within the scope it has been given.
4. From zero side-effects to real-world effects.

A model responding to a query cannot break anything — it only produces text. An agent that can write files, send emails, execute code, and call APIs can have permanent, irreversible consequences. This is where the power comes from, and also where the risk comes from.
Why This Shift Is Happening Now
Three enabling conditions converged around 2022–2023 to make the agentic paradigm practical:
Sufficiently capable base models.

GPT-4, Claude 2, and Gemini demonstrated that LLMs could reliably follow multi-step instructions, use tools, and recover from errors — capabilities that prior models lacked consistently. A model that fails 30% of tool calls cannot run a 20-step pipeline; a model that fails 3% of the time can.
Structured tool-use APIs.

OpenAI's function-calling (2023) and Anthropic's tool-use API gave developers a reliable way to expose callable functions to models. The model outputs structured JSON specifying the tool and its arguments; the host executes it. This removed the fragile "parse free-form text to find tool calls" step that made early agent frameworks unreliable.
Larger context windows.

Context windows grew from 4K tokens (GPT-3, 2020) to 200K (Claude 2.1, 2023) to 1M+ tokens (Gemini 1.5, 2024). Longer context means an agent can hold more of its working state in memory, execute longer tasks without truncation, and read entire codebases or document sets in a single call.
The New Mental Model: Delegation
The correct mental model for agentic AI is
delegation
— the same mental model you use when giving a task to a capable colleague.
When you delegate to a colleague, you:
State the goal clearly (what done looks like)
Provide context they need (constraints, prior work, relevant resources)
Grant access to the tools they need (files, accounts, systems)
Set a checkpoint cadence (check in every hour, or only when done)
Review and course-correct the output

This is exactly how you use an LLM agent effectively. The people who struggle with agents tend to use the old mental model: they expect to interact turn-by-turn, manually guide every step, and provide all intermediate details themselves. That pattern defeats the purpose of the agent.
Measuring the Shift: Automation Rate
One useful metric for the paradigm shift is the
automation rate

: what fraction of steps in a workflow does the human still need to perform? In the old paradigm, the human performs every step and the AI only assists with some. In the agentic paradigm, the agent performs most steps and the human validates the outcome.
Workflow
Old paradigm
Agentic paradigm
Fix a bug in a codebase
Human locates, asks AI for fix, manually applies, tests
Agent finds, fixes, tests; human reviews diff
Summarise 50 research papers
Human pastes each paper manually, reads 50 summaries
Agent fetches, summarises, synthesises; human reads synthesis
Monitor server logs for anomalies
Human manually queries logs, pastes excerpts
Agent runs continuously, alerts only on anomalies
Book travel with constraints
Human searches multiple sites, compares prices
Agent queries APIs, selects best option, confirms with human
The Human Role Changes — Not Disappears
A common misconception is that the agentic paradigm eliminates the human. It does not — it changes what the human does. Humans remain responsible for:
Goal setting.
Deciding what outcomes are worth pursuing. An agent optimises for the goal you gave it — if the goal is wrong, the outcome is wrong, no matter how competently the agent executed.
Scope and access control.

Deciding what tools and systems the agent is allowed to touch. An agent that can send emails on your behalf should only do so after explicit authorisation — not implicitly because you gave it a broad goal.
Oversight and verification.

Reviewing outcomes, especially for high-stakes or irreversible actions. The question is not "did the agent do something?" but "did the agent do the right thing?" Humans catch misalignment between the specified goal and the actual intent.
Accountability.
Legal and ethical responsibility for the agent's actions remains with the human who deployed it. "The AI did it" is not a defence.

The skilled use of agents is therefore not about eliminating human judgment — it is about directing human judgment toward higher-leverage decisions: what to build, for whom, within what constraints, and how to verify the result.
Coding the Shift: Before and After
Here is the same task — finding the most-referenced function in a Python repository — implemented in both paradigms:

# ─── OLD PARADIGM: AI assists each step ──────────────────────────────
# Step 1: Human manually lists files
import os
files = [f for f in os.listdir('.') if f.endswith('.py')]

# Step 2: Human asks AI "how do I count function calls in Python?"
# AI answers with a code snippet → human copies, adapts, runs it
import ast
from collections import Counter

def count_calls(source: str) -> Counter:
    tree = ast.parse(source)
    return Counter(
        node.func.id
        for node in ast.walk(tree)
        if isinstance(node, ast.Call)
        and isinstance(node.func, ast.Name)
    )

call_counts: Counter = Counter()
for filename in files:
    try:
        with open(filename) as f:
            call_counts.update(count_calls(f.read()))
    except SyntaxError:
        pass  # Human noticed this edge case on second run and added it

# Step 3: Human asks AI "how do I print the top 5 from a Counter?"
# AI answers → human applies
print(call_counts.most_common(5))

# Total human actions: 3 AI queries + manual editing + running + debugging


# ─── NEW PARADIGM: Agent handles the whole task ───────────────────────
# Human writes one instruction (as a system prompt or user message):

INSTRUCTION = """
Find the most-referenced function in this Python repository.
Steps you should take:
1. List all .py files in the current directory.
2. Parse each file and count all function call expressions.
3. Report the top 5 most-called functions with their counts.
Handle syntax errors gracefully by skipping malformed files.
Return a numbered list.
"""

# Agent receives this, calls tools (read_file, run_code), and returns:
# 1. os.path.join  — called 47 times
# 2. print         — called 31 times
# 3. open          — called 28 times
# 4. len           — called 19 times
# 5. isinstance    — called 14 times

# Total human actions: write one clear goal. Review output.

The code itself is similar — AST walking, Counter, most_common. The difference is who does each step: in the old paradigm the human drives every micro-step; in the new paradigm the agent executes the full chain autonomously given a clear goal specification.
New Risks the Shift Introduces
Every paradigm shift creates new failure modes. The agentic paradigm introduces risks that did not exist when AI was a query-response tool:
Goal misspecification.

When the agent executes only one step, a wrong instruction produces one wrong sentence. When the agent executes 50 steps, a wrong goal specification produces 50 wrong actions — all coherently optimised toward the wrong end.
Scope creep.

An agent pursuing a goal will try to acquire whatever resources it determines are helpful — files, accounts, permissions, sub-agents. Without explicit scope constraints, it may touch systems the deployer never intended.
Irreversibility.

An agent that deleted files, sent emails, or made API calls cannot be "un-run." The asymmetry between the cost of a wrong action and the cost of pausing to verify drives a strong preference for human checkpoints before irreversible steps.
Error amplification.

An early error in a multi-step task is silently propagated through all subsequent steps. The agent may produce a confident, well-formatted final output that is entirely built on a wrong assumption from step 2.

These risks are manageable — humans manage them when delegating to colleagues every day. The solution is the same: clear goal specification, appropriate scope limits, checkpoints for high-stakes actions, and output review. The techniques are new; the discipline is ancient.
Designing Systems for the Agentic Paradigm
Building software for agentic AI requires rethinking several design decisions that were settled in the tool paradigm. Here are the most consequential ones:
Idempotent operations where possible.

When an agent can re-try a step after failure, idempotent operations (calling them twice has the same effect as calling them once) prevent duplicate side-effects. Writing to a file with a fixed path is idempotent. Appending to a log is not. Design APIs and tool interfaces to be idempotent whenever the operation's semantics allow it.
Checkpointing and resumability.

Long tasks can fail partway through — API timeout, network error, context window exhausted. A well-designed agentic system persists intermediate state so it can resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch. Represent task progress as a serialisable data structure, not as in-memory state.
Explicit task boundaries.

Define a clear contract for each task: inputs (what the agent receives), outputs (what it must produce), success criteria (how to verify the output is correct), and termination conditions (when to stop even if the goal is not fully achieved). Vague task boundaries lead to unbounded execution and unfalsifiable outcomes.
Least-privilege tool access.

Give the agent only the tools it needs for the specific task, not all tools available. An agent summarising documents should not have a tool to delete files. Scope tool permissions per task, not per agent, to minimise the blast radius of errors.
Structured output validation.

When the agent's output must conform to a schema (e.g. JSON, a typed data structure), validate it programmatically before consuming it. The agent may produce plausibly-formatted output that fails schema validation — catching this at the boundary prevents corrupted data from propagating to downstream systems.

import json
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Optional
import time

# ─── Checkpoint-aware task runner ─────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class TaskState:
    task_id: str
    goal: str
    steps_completed: list[str]
    current_step: int
    result: Optional[str] = None
    error: Optional[str] = None
    status: str = "in_progress"   # in_progress | done | failed

class CheckpointedTaskRunner:
    def __init__(self, checkpoint_dir: str = "/tmp/agent_checkpoints"):
        self.checkpoint_dir = Path(checkpoint_dir)
        self.checkpoint_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

    def _checkpoint_path(self, task_id: str) -> Path:
        return self.checkpoint_dir / f"{task_id}.json"

    def save_checkpoint(self, state: TaskState) -> None:
        path = self._checkpoint_path(state.task_id)
        path.write_text(json.dumps(asdict(state), indent=2))
        print(f"  [checkpoint] saved step {state.current_step}")

    def load_checkpoint(self, task_id: str) -> Optional[TaskState]:
        path = self._checkpoint_path(task_id)
        if path.exists():
            data = json.loads(path.read_text())
            print(f"  [checkpoint] resuming from step {data['current_step']}")
            return TaskState(**data)
        return None

    def run(self, task_id: str, goal: str, steps: list[callable]) -> TaskState:
        # Resume from checkpoint if one exists
        state = self.load_checkpoint(task_id) or TaskState(
            task_id=task_id,
            goal=goal,
            steps_completed=[],
            current_step=0,
        )

        if state.status == "done":
            print("  [task already complete]")
            return state

        for i, step_fn in enumerate(steps):
            if i < state.current_step:
                continue   # skip already-completed steps

            print(f"  Running step {i}: {step_fn.__name__}")
            try:
                result = step_fn(state)
                state.steps_completed.append(f"{step_fn.__name__}: {result}")
                state.current_step = i + 1
                self.save_checkpoint(state)
            except Exception as e:
                state.error = str(e)
                state.status = "failed"
                self.save_checkpoint(state)
                print(f"  Step {i} failed: {e}")
                return state

        state.status = "done"
        state.result = state.steps_completed[-1] if state.steps_completed else ""
        self.save_checkpoint(state)
        return state


# ─── Example task steps ───────────────────────────────────────────────
def fetch_data(state: TaskState) -> str:
    time.sleep(0.01)   # simulate API call
    return "fetched 42 records"

def process_data(state: TaskState) -> str:
    time.sleep(0.01)
    return "processed into 38 valid records"

def write_output(state: TaskState) -> str:
    return "wrote report.json (38 records)"

runner = CheckpointedTaskRunner()
final = runner.run(
    task_id="task-001",
    goal="fetch, process, and report sales data",
    steps=[fetch_data, process_data, write_output],
)
print(f"\nFinal status: {final.status}")
print(f"Result: {final.result}")
Agent Autonomy Levels: A Practical Taxonomy

Not every agentic system needs full autonomy. Choosing the right autonomy level for a task depends on its stakes, reversibility, and how often the agent's judgment can be trusted. Here is a practical taxonomy:
Level
Human role
Use when
Example
L1 — Suggestion
Approves every action
High stakes, unfamiliar domain
GitHub Copilot inline suggestions
L2 — Supervised
Reviews before irreversible steps
Mixed reversible/irreversible task
Claude Code with permission prompts
L3 — Semi-autonomous
Periodic check-ins
Long tasks in trusted environments
CI agent that runs and reports
L4 — Fully autonomous
Reviews final outcome only
Well-bounded, reversible task
Nightly data pipeline, log monitor

In practice, a single agentic workflow often spans multiple levels: the agent runs autonomously at L4 for most steps, escalates to L2 when it needs to send an email or delete data, and asks the human to decide at L1 when it encounters genuine ambiguity. Design your agent's escalation triggers explicitly rather than defaulting to full autonomy or full supervision.
Writing Effective Goal Specifications
The quality of the goal specification is the largest single determinant of agent success. A well-written goal specification answers four questions explicitly:
What is done?
The success criterion. Not "improve the code" but "all tests pass and coverage is above 80%." The agent needs a verifiable termination condition.
What is in scope?
Which files, systems, APIs, and data the agent may touch. Explicit inclusion is safer than implicit exclusion.
What must not happen?
Hard constraints. "Do not delete any files." "Do not send any emails without confirmation." "Do not modify the production database." Negative constraints are as important as positive ones.
What is the output format?
How the agent should deliver its result — a file path, a JSON object, a git diff, a summary paragraph. Unstructured outputs require more human post-processing.

# ── Poor goal specification ──────────────────────────────────────────
"Improve the user authentication code"

# Problems:
# • No success criterion — when is it "improved" enough?
# • No scope — which files? Which auth system?
# • No constraints — can it change the database schema?
# • No output format — what should it return?

# ── Good goal specification ──────────────────────────────────────────
"""
Goal: Refactor the password-hashing logic in src/auth/password.py

Success criteria:
- All existing tests in tests/test_password.py continue to pass
- The bcrypt library (already installed) replaces the current SHA-256 approach
- The public API (hash_password, verify_password) remains unchanged

Scope:
- You may read any file in src/ and tests/
- You may modify only src/auth/password.py
- Do NOT modify any other files

Constraints:
- Do not change the function signatures of hash_password() or verify_password()
- Do not commit any changes — produce only a git diff

Output:
- Print the unified diff of the changes you would make
- If the tests pass with your changes, say PASS; otherwise say FAIL with details
"""

# This specification gives the agent:
# ✓ A clear terminal state (tests pass, API unchanged)
# ✓ Explicit scope (one file)
# ✓ Hard constraints (no signature changes, no commit)
# ✓ Unambiguous output format (diff + PASS/FAIL)

Writing clear goal specifications is a skill. It transfers directly from writing good tickets, bug reports, and test cases — the agentic paradigm does not require new abilities, but it amplifies the consequences of doing it poorly.
When NOT to Use the Agentic Paradigm

The agentic paradigm is not always the right choice. Its overhead — multiple LLM calls, tool execution latency, potential for cascading errors — makes it a poor fit for tasks that the tool paradigm handles well:
Single-step lookups.
"What is the capital of France?" needs one LLM call. Wrapping it in an agent loop adds latency without benefit.
Fully deterministic pipelines.

If the sequence of steps is known in advance and does not depend on intermediate results, a standard scripted pipeline is faster, cheaper, and more reliable than an agent that re-decides the same sequence on every run.
Tasks where correctness must be formally verified.

An agent generating cryptographic code, nuclear plant control logic, or aircraft autopilot firmware must be verified by formal methods — no amount of "the agent seemed confident" substitutes for proof. Agents are currently unsuitable as the sole author of safety-critical code.
Cost-sensitive high-volume tasks.

If a task runs a million times per day, the cost per agentic run (multiple API calls, tool invocations) may be prohibitive. Distill the agent's behaviour into a fine-tuned model or a rule-based system once the agent has demonstrated what the correct behaviour looks like.

The agentic paradigm excels at tasks that are: open-ended (the steps cannot be scripted in advance), information-rich (the agent needs to gather and integrate data from multiple sources), and low-frequency enough that per-run cost is acceptable. When all three conditions hold, an agent is the right tool. When any one fails, reconsider.
Summary
The agentic paradigm shift reframes AI from a query-response tool to an autonomous goal-pursuer: the human specifies what to achieve, the agent decides how.
Four concrete changes: answers → outcomes; single-turn → multi-step; passive → active; zero side-effects → real-world effects.
Three enabling conditions made this practical: capable base models, structured tool-use APIs, and long context windows.
The correct mental model is delegation: state the goal, provide context, grant access, set checkpoints, review the outcome.
Humans remain responsible for goal setting, scope control, oversight, and accountability — the agent's competence does not transfer accountability.
New risks introduced: goal misspecification, scope creep, irreversibility, and error amplification — all manageable with clear specification and appropriate checkpoints.
